phrasal_verbs.meaning: say something suddenly and without thinking about the effect it will have, usually because you are nervous or excited
become available to buy or see; become easy to notice; become known; be spoken, heard, or understood in a particular way
say something suddenly, usually something that surprises or shocks people
stop talking because you have forgotten what you were going to say
if news gets (a)round, a lot of people hear it
be connected to a place by telephone; make someone understand what you are trying to say
talk about something that is intended to be a secret
give someone something, for example a message, that someone else has given you
explain an idea, belief, etc in a way that is easy to understand
write something on a piece of paper so that it will not be forgotten and can be looked at later; state officially how something should be done
make it difficult to hear what someone says by shouting while they are speaking
state your opinion firmly and publicly about something, especially in order to protest against or defend something
discuss a problem or a plan
succeed in persuading someone to agree something; discuss something in a general way and without dealing with the most important issues

argument: have / get into an argument (with sb)
ask: ask a lot / too much (of sb)
notice: at short / a moment's / a few hours' notice
paper: not worth the paper it's written/printed on
pen: the pen is mightier than the sword
talk: talk your way into / out of (doing)
word: put in a (good) word for sb
write: have sth / be written all over your face

A.title: Complete using the correct form of the words
A.instructions: Complete each sentence by using the correct form of the words in the boxes.

questions.sentence: In my view, Brian is ...... the scale of the problem. The actual figures are far lower than he claims.

It's fine to be proud of your achievements, but there's no need to ...... about them at every opportunity.
The photographer was just ...... Mia when he said the portrait made her look twenty years younger.

Clara has always been someone you can ...... in, especially during difficult periods.
We are delighted to ...... that your application for funding has been approved.
No personal information will be ...... to third parties without your explicit consent.
Words alone cannot ...... the depth of gratitude we feel towards the rescue team.
The delegates were ...... among themselves, but nobody dared challenge the chairperson openly.
Even the most experienced presenter ...... over their lines from time to time.
He stood at the podium, ...... nervously as he tried to recall his opening line.
Please don't ...... when addressing the audience. Speak clearly and project your voice!
Half the office was ...... about that podcast on digital privacy. They said it was absolutely unmissable.
Stop ...... in the margins of the textbooks – those copies belong to the library.

Every time the topic of press freedom comes up, my neighbour ...... for hours without pause.
Here's a practical ...... for any aspiring journalist: always verify your sources before publishing.

B.title: Choose the correct word
B.instructions: Choose the correct word to complete each sentence.

questions.sentence: Are you frustrated by technical **jargon / slang** in official financial documents?
...that seems almost impossible to fully **comprehend / contradict**?
Do you notice politicians using **vague / relevant** language to dodge difficult questions?
...or deliberately **literal / ambiguous** phrasing to avoid making firm commitments?
...and realise you haven't got a/an **inkling / hint** of what they are actually trying to say?
Is your doctor's handwriting on prescriptions completely **colloquial / illegible** to you?
We **declare / denounce** all forms of communication designed to mislead rather than inform.
Let's be **blunt / sharp** about the situation.

A volunteer handed me a colourful ...... about the history of the local community media house.

questions.sentence: If you master the craft of character and plot, you could become a celebrated (1) ......

E.title: Write one word in each gap (Phrasal verbs)
E.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: This is strictly confidential, so please don't ...... on to anyone at the office.
The newsletter ...... out on the first Monday of every month.
Information ...... around incredibly quickly in the digital age.
Everyone was astonished when Sophie ...... out with the announcement that she was retiring.
The new director will need to ...... her vision for the company across to the board clearly.
The committee has ...... down strict guidelines on how all complaints should be handled.
Most people in the audience ...... on straight away that the speaker was being ironic.

F.title: Phrasal verbs with down
F.instructions: Tick the phrasal verbs which are also connected to the idea of 'defeating someone or being defeated'.

questions.sentence: The councillor backed down when she saw the strength of public opposition to the proposal.
You'll need to bend down to reach the parcel on the bottom shelf.
The soloist almost broke down in tears during the final, deeply emotional movement.
A vote of no confidence could bring down the entire administration.
The authorities are clamping down on websites that spread deliberate misinformation.
The minister was forced to climb down after the leaked documents proved him wrong.
The editor decided to crack down on reporters who consistently failed to verify their sources.
She gobbled down her lunch in five minutes and rushed back to the newsroom.
Half the production crew went down with a stomach bug on the day of the live broadcast.
I'll jot those figures down while you continue explaining the rest of the plan.
My camera let me down at the worst possible moment, seconds before the press conference began.
After weeks of resistance, he finally managed to wear the management down.

...... answer to your enquiry, we would be happy to supply a full written transcript.
Sitting down and actually putting pen ...... paper was by far the hardest part of writing the book.
Put your rough ideas down ...... paper first, and we'll discuss them in detail at the next meeting.
You've just received some fantastic news, haven't you? It's written all ...... your face!

H.title: Key word transformation
H.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning, using the word given. Use between three and eight words.

questions.sentence:
The editorial pieces look identical to the adverts on this page. **difference**
It's virtually impossible ...... the adverts on this page.

You need to persuade the editor not to run that controversial photograph. **out**

J.title: Correct the wrong words in bold

K.title: Word formation (suffixes and prefixes)
K.instructions: Complete each sentence by changing the form of the word in capitals.

questions.sentence: He stood there completely ......, making it impossible to judge how the interview had gone. **EXPRESS**
Major streaming platforms spend enormous sums on ...... for their new original releases. **PUBLIC**
The awards ceremony was ...... tedious – not a single genuine moment of surprise all evening. **SPEAK**
I have some reservations about the ...... of this headline; it could easily be misread. **WORD**
The trouble with inviting him onto a live programme is that he is far too ...... to manage easily. **TALK**
Try to avoid using ...... marks in formal journalistic or academic writing. **EXCLAIM**
Any presenter who covers the same format for too long risks becoming hopelessly ...... **TYPE**
Advertisers specifically target audiences they know to be highly ...... **SUGGEST**
Several of the reader letters contained language too offensive and ...... for publication. **PRINT**
The editor was extremely ...... that every single fact be independently checked before going to print. **INSIST**

M.title: Complete the sentences with over- words

questions.sentence: The vegetables were slightly ...... and had lost most of their natural flavour.
I turned up in a suit and tie, but everyone else was in casual clothes – I felt completely ......
We have significantly ...... on the advertising budget this quarter and urgently need to cut back.
Please calm down – you're getting ...... and it's affecting your ability to focus.
The reporter ...... the mark by publishing personal details that were never intended for public release.
The garden at the back of the studio was completely ...... and clearly hadn't been touched in years.
The parcel was ...... so the post office charged an additional handling fee.

N.title: Word formation (Rhetoric text)
N.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
N.passage: The (1) ______ 'the pen is mightier than the sword' might well have been coined with *Language Under Attack* in mind. Its author, Barker, is an (2) ______ defender of plain English and has made a bold (3) ______ of intent to expose the spread of empty, (4) ______ corporate language in public life. In his earlier (5) _______, Barker was already critical of the overuse of the passive voice, but it was not until a careless (6) ______ cut a key paragraph from his draft that he decided to (7) ______ his entire argument more forcefully. The result is a powerful (8) ______ of principles that runs throughout the whole book. The (9) ______ is clear: if we allow language to become hollow, we lose the capacity to think critically. Some have dismissed this as little more than (10) _______, but the evidence Barker provides is difficult to ignore.

questions.sentence: ...is an (2) ...... (SPEAK) defender
...has made a bold (3) ...... (DECLARE) of intent
...the spread of empty, (4) ...... (MEAN) corporate language
...in his earlier (5) ...... (WRITE)
...a careless (6) ...... (EDIT) cut a key paragraph
...decided to (7) ...... (PHRASE) his argument
...a powerful (8) ...... (STATE) of principles
The (9) ...... (IMPLY) is clear
...dismissed as little more than (10) ...... (HEAR)
